excel_structure.notes: Excel file contains 1 test case with complex multi-step flow. The Steps column contains embedded expected results that need to be parsed. Test case involves HP Instant Ink subscription pause plan functionality with visual and functional validations.

test_case_metadata.title: Billing Cycle Period card
test_case_metadata.description: Test case to verify the Billing Cycle Period card behavior on Print and Payment History page when a subscription plan is paused, including verification of complimentary pages, additional pages, progress bars, tooltips, and page count displays.
test_case_metadata.sub_module: Print and Payment History - Pause Plan
test_case_metadata.author: Nitish

custom_fields.section: Print and Payment History page with 25/50/100/300/700 pages plan
custom_fields.section_hierarchy: Instant Supply > Regression Test > 2 - Medium > Dashboard - Extended > Pause Plan > Print and Payment History page with 25/50/100/300/700 pages plan
custom_fields.analysis_status: To be discussed: Specific Ink printer? 4.Click the Pause Plan button on CIT modal or "Pause Instant Ink" link to trigger the pause plan. Manual execution for pages on overview to be performed and verified the same

preconditions.raw_text: Enroll the Ink printer with 50 pages plan or higher.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: A test HP Ink printer must be enrolled in the HP Instant Ink subscription service with a plan of at least 50 pages per month. This requires either API-based enrollment setup or manual enrollment before test execution. The printer must be registered, connected, and associated with a valid HP account.

implied_components.reason: Need API helper to enroll a printer with specific plan (50+ pages) for test data setup
Need printer serial number, account credentials, and plan details for enrollment
Need to verify that printer enrollment was successful before proceeding with test
Need utility to retrieve enrolled printer details for verification

preconditions.raw_text: Make sure the Subscription has no free months info.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: The HP Instant Ink subscription associated with the enrolled printer must not have any free trial months or promotional free months active. The subscription must be in a standard paid state. This may require checking subscription details via API or UI and ensuring no free month promotions are applied.

implied_components.reason: Need API to retrieve subscription details and verify no free months
Need assertion to confirm subscription has no free months info
Need utility to parse and validate subscription state

preconditions.raw_text: Make sure the subscription is "subscribed" status.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: The HP Instant Ink subscription must be in active 'subscribed' status (not paused, not cancelled, not expired, not pending). This requires verification of subscription status via API or dashboard UI before test execution.

implied_components.reason: Need API to check subscription status is 'subscribed'
Need assertion to verify subscription is in subscribed state
May need to verify subscription status on dashboard UI

preconditions.raw_text: Click the Pause Plan button on CIT modal or "Pause Instant Ink" link to trigger the pause plan.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: Before the main test steps begin, the user must navigate to the location where the Pause Plan functionality is available (either a CIT modal or a page with 'Pause Instant Ink' link), locate the pause plan button/link, click it, and complete the pause plan flow. This changes the subscription state from 'subscribed' to 'paused'. This is a critical setup step that must be completed successfully.

implied_components.reason: Need page object for CIT modal if pause is triggered from modal
Need page object for the page containing Pause Instant Ink link
Need locator for Pause Plan button on CIT modal
Need locator for Pause Instant Ink link
Need action method to click pause plan button
Need composite action to complete entire pause plan process
Need assertion to verify plan was paused successfully
Need wait utility for pause confirmation message or state change

This requires either direct URL navigation or menu navigation through HP Instant Ink section to reach Print and Payment History page. After navigation, wait for the page to fully load and verify that the page is displayed correctly.

Click on Print and Payment History link/menu item

test_steps.raw_action: Check the Billing Cycle Period card.
test_steps.raw_expected_result: Verify the plan pause information is not displayed.
test_steps.interpreted_action: Locate the Billing Cycle Period card component on the Print and Payment History page. This card is a UI component that displays billing cycle information. At this point in the test flow (before 32 days shift and billing charge), the expected behavior is that plan pause information should NOT be displayed on this card. This requires locating the card, verifying it is visible, and then asserting that no pause plan information is shown within the card.

sub_actions.action: Check for absence of pause plan information

test_steps.raw_action: Even shift 32 days and trigger billing charge.
test_steps.interpreted_action: This step requires manipulating the system date/time or subscription billing cycle to simulate 32 days passing. This is typically done through API calls to backend services or database manipulation to advance the billing cycle date by 32 days. After shifting the date, trigger the billing charge process (which may be automatic or may require triggering via API). This is a complex test data setup step that changes system state.

test_steps.raw_action: Print some pages(less than the plan, such as 6 pages). Refresh the dashboard page.
test_steps.raw_expected_result: Verify the "Complimentary pages" progress bar is filled with the black color and the value is displayed as correct (such as "6 of 10 used").
test_steps.interpreted_action: This step requires triggering a print action to print a specific number of pages (6 pages in the example, which is less than the 10 complimentary pages available). This is likely done through API call to simulate printing or through printer simulation. After printing, refresh the dashboard/page to see updated page count. Then verify that the Complimentary pages progress bar is filled proportionally with black color and the value text updates to show '6 of 10 used'.

test_steps.raw_action: Continue to print some pages(more than the plan, such as 15 pages). Refresh the dashboard page.
test_steps.raw_expected_result: Verify the progress bars for "Complimentary pages" and "Additional pages" are displayed well.
test_steps.interpreted_action: Continue printing more pages beyond the complimentary limit. In this example, print a total of 15 pages (which means 10 complimentary pages are fully used and 5 additional pages are used). After printing, refresh the page. Now verify that TWO progress bars are displayed: one for Complimentary pages (fully filled) and one for Additional pages (partially filled with 5 out of 10 used). This tests the overflow behavior when complimentary pages are exceeded.

flow_sequence.action_summary: Navigate to Print and Payment History page and verify initial state (no pause info displayed)
Shift date by 32 days and trigger billing charge
Return to Print and Payment History page and verify pause info is now displayed
Verify Complimentary pages progress bar, tooltip, and initial values
Print 6 pages and verify Complimentary pages progress bar updates
Print additional pages to exceed limit and verify Additional pages section appears
Verify Additional pages progress bar, color, value, tooltip, and messages

flow_analysis.critical_path: Navigate to Print and Payment History → Verify no pause info → Shift date 32 days → Verify pause info displayed → Verify Complimentary pages section → Print 6 pages → Verify updated values → Print to 15 pages total → Verify Additional pages section appears → Verify all Additional pages elements

error_paths.expected_behavior: Test fails - cannot test page count updates

Subscription must be in subscribed status with no free months
Plan must be paused before test execution

API access for date shifting and print simulation must be available
teardown_requirements: Test data (printed pages) may need to be reset for subsequent tests

automation_notes.complexity_justification: This test case is very complex due to multiple factors: (1) Requires backend date manipulation and billing trigger via API, (2) Involves responsive testing with different interactions for desktop vs mobile/tablet, (3) Requires visual verification of progress bar colors and fill percentages, (4) Tests dynamic UI behavior where Additional pages section appears conditionally, (5) Involves cumulative state tracking across multiple print operations, (6) Requires extensive precondition setup including printer enrollment and plan pause, (7) Contains 15 detailed test steps with multiple assertions per step, (8) Involves tooltip interactions with hover/click based on device type, (9) Requires precise text matching including special characters and formatting, (10) Tests both positive and negative scenarios (pause info not displayed, then displayed).

risks.risk: Backend API dependencies - Test heavily relies on APIs for date shifting, billing trigger, and print simulation. If these APIs are not available or unstable, test cannot execute.
risks.mitigation: Ensure test environment has dedicated test APIs for date manipulation and print simulation. Implement robust API error handling and retry logic.

risks.risk: Dynamic element appearance - Additional pages section appears only after exceeding complimentary limit. Timing issues may cause flakiness.
risks.mitigation: Use explicit waits with proper conditions for dynamic element appearance. Implement retry logic for element detection.

risks.risk: Visual verification complexity - Test requires verifying progress bar colors (black vs yellow) and fill percentages. CSS property checks may be fragile across browsers.
risks.mitigation: Use robust CSS property extraction methods. Consider using visual regression testing tools for color verification. Test across multiple browsers.

risks.risk: Responsive testing complexity - Different interactions for desktop (hover) vs mobile/tablet (click) increase test complexity and maintenance burden.
risks.mitigation: Implement device detection utility and parameterized test execution. Use conditional logic for device-specific interactions.

risks.risk: State dependency across steps - Test has strong dependencies between steps (cumulative page count). Failure in early steps blocks all subsequent steps.
risks.mitigation: Implement proper test step isolation where possible. Use setup/teardown to ensure clean state. Add checkpoints after critical steps.

risks.risk: Precondition setup complexity - Test requires extensive precondition setup (enrollment, subscription verification, plan pause). Setup failures will block test execution.
risks.mitigation: Implement robust precondition setup with verification. Use API-based setup where possible for speed and reliability. Add detailed logging for setup steps.

risks.risk: Text matching precision - Test requires exact text matching including special characters like '(Pause Plan)' and specific formatting. Localization or UI text changes will break assertions.
risks.mitigation: Use flexible text matching (contains instead of equals) where appropriate. Externalize expected text values to test data files for easy updates.

risks.risk: Tooltip interaction timing - Tooltips may have animation delays or may not appear consistently, especially on hover interactions.
risks.mitigation: Add explicit waits for tooltip appearance. Implement retry logic for tooltip interactions. Consider using JavaScript to trigger tooltip display.

risks.risk: Page refresh timing - Test involves multiple page refreshes to see updated data. Backend data sync delays may cause stale data to be displayed.
risks.mitigation: Add waits after page refresh to allow backend data sync. Implement polling mechanism to verify data has updated before proceeding.

risks.risk: Progress bar calculation accuracy - Calculating progress bar fill percentage from CSS width may have rounding errors or browser-specific variations.
risks.mitigation: Use tolerance-based assertions for percentage comparisons. Verify both visual fill and text value for consistency.

recommendations: Implement Page Object Model with PrintAndPaymentHistoryPage as the main page object containing all locators and action methods for this page.
Use API-based test data setup for printer enrollment, subscription creation, and plan pause to reduce test execution time and increase reliability.
Implement a dedicated test utility layer for backend operations (date shift, billing trigger, print simulation) with proper error handling and logging.
Create a responsive testing framework that detects device type and executes appropriate interaction methods (hover vs click) automatically.
Implement visual verification utilities for progress bar color and fill percentage checks using CSS property extraction.
Use explicit waits with custom expected conditions for all dynamic element appearances, especially for Additional pages section.
Externalize all expected text values, colors, and numerical values to test data files for easy maintenance and localization support.
Implement comprehensive logging at each test step to aid debugging, especially for API calls and backend state changes.
Consider breaking this test into smaller, more focused tests: (1) Pause info display test, (2) Complimentary pages test, (3) Additional pages overflow test.
Implement screenshot capture on every assertion failure with clear naming convention including step number and assertion type.
Use data-driven testing approach to test with different page counts and plan types (50, 100, 300, 700 pages plans).
Implement test data cleanup in teardown to reset page counts and subscription state for subsequent test runs.
Consider using visual regression testing tools (like Percy or Applitools) for progress bar visual verification instead of CSS property checks.
Implement retry mechanism for flaky steps, especially tooltip interactions and page refresh operations.
Add performance monitoring to track page load times and API response times to identify bottlenecks.
special_handling_notes: This test requires special test environment with APIs for date manipulation and print simulation. Production environment cannot be used.
Test involves responsive testing - must be executed on both desktop and mobile/tablet viewports to cover all interaction paths.
Progress bar color verification requires CSS property checks - ensure WebDriver has capability to extract computed styles.
Test modifies backend state (date, billing cycle, page count) - ensure proper cleanup to avoid affecting other tests.
Tooltip interactions may behave differently across browsers - cross-browser testing is essential.
Test execution time will be longer due to multiple page refreshes and API calls - consider timeout configurations.
Screenshot references in test case (index.php?/attachments/get/35447537) suggest visual validation is important - consider manual visual review in addition to automated checks.
Test case mentions 'To be discussed' items in Analysis Status - clarify specific printer requirements and manual verification needs before full automation.
Complimentary pages limit is 10 during pause plan - this may be configurable, consider parameterizing this value.
Additional pages are charged in blocks of 10 - verify this business rule is correctly implemented in assertions.
automation_notes.framework_compatibility_notes: This test case analysis is structured for the Aura automation framework. All component names follow Aura naming conventions (PascalCase for page objects, snake_case for methods and locators). The analysis assumes Aura framework provides: (1) Page Object Model support, (2) API testing capabilities, (3) Explicit wait utilities, (4) Screenshot capture on failure, (5) Test data management, (6) Configuration management, (7) Logging framework, (8) Responsive testing support. Additional framework extensions may be needed for: (1) CSS property extraction utilities, (2) Progress bar percentage calculation, (3) Device type detection, (4) Tooltip interaction helpers, (5) Visual regression testing integration.
